title: Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
oneLiner: Life-threatening UGI bleed from ruptured esophageal/gastric varices due to portal hypertension; requires immediate resuscitation and endoscopic therapy.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 52-year-old male with known alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) presents to ED with massive hematemesis (two cups of bright red blood). He is pale, sweaty, and restless. Heart rate 115 bpm, BP 85/55, saturations 96% on air. Abdominal exam reveals mild ascites and a firm liver edge. Skin shows spider naevi and palmar erythema. He has not had an endoscopy before. On rectal exam – melena.

**Dissect the vignette:**

- **Massive hematemesis (bright red blood)** → high-volume arterial-like bleeding from high-pressure variceal source (not oozing). Bright red implies fresh, not altered by gastric acid – suggests ongoing active bleeding.
- **Known alcoholic cirrhosis** → the underlying cause of portal hypertension – varices are present in ~50% of cirrhotics at diagnosis.
- **Hemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension)** → hypovolemic shock from acute blood loss – needs immediate IV access and resuscitation.
- **Ascites + firm liver edge** → signs of portal hypertension and chronic liver disease (fibrosis). Ascites indicates decompensation – worse prognosis.
- **Spider naevi / palmar erythema** → stigmata of chronic liver disease – confirm portal hypertensive aetiology.
- **Melena** → blood has transited through the GI tract – but here massive hematemesis dominates. Even with upper GI bleed, rapid transit can produce melena.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Only melena, no hematemesis** – can happen if bleeding is slow or intermittent, or if the varices are gastric (fundal). The junior doctor might focus on lower GI source – always consider UGI in any melena.
- **Patient with no known liver disease** – first presentation of cirrhosis can be variceal bleeding. No stigmata on exam. Be alert for history of alcohol use, hepatitis, obesity; check LFTs and platelet count (low platelets suggest portal hypertension / hypersplenism).
- **Bleeding after non-invasive procedure (e.g., PEG insertion, endoscopy for other indication)** – acute increase in portal pressure due to sedation or straining can rupture a previously silent varix. Don't dismiss as just "post-procedure ooze."
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
Variceal upper GI bleeding is a severe hemorrhagic event from dilated, tortuous veins (varices) in the oesophagus or stomach that develop because of high blood pressure in the portal venous system (portal hypertension). These veins are fragile and prone to rupture, leading to massive, life-threatening blood loss.

**Why it matters clinically – frequency, stakes, common pitfalls:**

- **High stakes:** 6-week mortality of variceal bleeding is 15–25% per episode. Each rebleed event worsens survival. It is the second most common cause of upper GI bleed after peptic ulcer, but far more dangerous.
- **Frequency:** Most common in patients with cirrhosis (global burden: ~2 million deaths/year from cirrhosis, ~30% will have varices; 30% will bleed at some point). Also seen in extrahepatic portal vein thrombosis.
- **Common pitfalls:** Delayed fluid resuscitation (over-resuscitation worsens portal pressure); failing to start vasoactive drugs immediately (before endoscopy); forgetting antibiotics (reduce infection and rebleeding); not considering TIPS early in uncontrolled bleeding.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**

Portal hypertension is defined as a hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) >10 mmHg. In cirrhosis, fibrosis and regenerative nodules compress hepatic sinusoids, increasing intrahepatic resistance. Additionally, splanchnic vasodilation (due to nitric oxide, VEGF) increases portal blood flow, exacerbating hypertension. Over time, the body tries to decompress the portal system by opening collateral vessels – gastroesophageal (coronary vein → azygos), splenorenal, caput medusae. These collaterals are thin-walled and lack smooth muscle. As HVPG exceeds 12 mmHg, varices develop; at HVPG >16 mmHg, they are at high risk of rupture.

**Mechanism → sign:**

- **Rupture → hematemesis / melena:** Increased wall tension (LaPlace's law: T = P × r / thickness). Larger varices (red wale signs) rupture more easily. Acid reflux + mechanical trauma from swallowing can erode the overlying mucosa.
- **Hemodynamic instability:** Acute loss of 30–40% circulating volume → compensatory tachycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries. Liver disease blunts the normal vascular response – worse shock.
- **Ascites and spider naevi:** Portal hypertension increases hydrostatic pressure in splanchnic capillaries, causing fluid shift into peritoneal cavity. Spider naevi are caused by oestrogen accumulation (liver can't metabolise) leading to dilated arterioles – marker of chronicity.
- **Thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism:** Splenic congestion → sequestration of platelets – low platelet count is a clue to portal hypertension before varices are visible.
- **Encephalopathy:** Blood in the gut is a nitrogen load – bacteria produce ammonia, which crosses the blood-brain barrier → impaired consciousness, asterixis. This can worsen after bleeding.

**Connect to pattern cues:**
Patient with cirrhosis + melena + no pain → think variceal (painless massive bleed). Peptic ulcer often has EPIGASTRIC PAIN. Mallory-Weiss has history of retching.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Chronic liver disease (most common)** | Cirrhosis (alcoholic, viral hepatitis B/C, NAFLD, autoimmune, PBC, PSC) | Known liver disease, stigmata, abnormal LFTs, low platelets |
| **Extrahepatic portal vein obstruction** | Portal vein thrombosis (neonatal, pancreatitis, hypercoagulable, myeloproliferative) | Present with variceal bleed but normal liver function; splenomegaly out of proportion |
| **Pre-hepatic (splenic vein thrombosis)** | Pancreatitis, pancreatic tumour → isolated left-sided (gastric) varices | Oesophageal varices may be absent; gastric fundal varices; normal liver |
| **Post-hepatic (Budd-Chiari, VOD)** | Hepatic vein outflow obstruction (hypercoagulable, myeloproliferative) | Hepatomegaly, ascites, acute onset with elevated transaminases; liver may not be cirrhotic initially |
| **Triggers of rupture** | Increased portal pressure (straining, ascites paracentesis, constipation, heavy meal); infection (SBP); NSAIDs/aspirin; alcohol binge | Recent event that raised intra-abdominal pressure or caused mucosal damage |

**Most common cause:** Alcoholic cirrhosis (or now NAFLD rising).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic scoring:** Use the **Rockall score** (pre-endoscopy: age, shock, comorbidities) or **GBS (Glasgow-Blatchford)** to predict need for intervention. But for varices specifically – **endoscopy** is the gold standard diagnostic and therapeutic tool.

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Full blood count** | Haemoglobin, platelets | Assess severity of anaemia, detect thrombocytopenia suggesting hypersplenism | Hb drop of >2 g/dL = substantial bleed; Platelet <100 = likely portal HTN |
| **Urea & creatinine** | Raised urea relative to creatinine (with normal renal function) indicates blood in gut (urea load from protein digestion) | Differentiates upper vs lower GI bleed – uraemia from upper bleed | BUN/Cr ratio >30:1 suggests UGI source (not specific but helpful in context) |
| **LFTs + INR** | Albumin low, bilirubin elevated, INR prolonged | Assess liver synthetic function – guides prognosis (Child-Pugh); high INR increases bleeding risk and is a marker of severity | INR >2 = risk of rebleeding; Child-Pugh C = poor prognosis |
| **Chest X-ray** | Aspiration pneumonitis, free air | Rule out perforation (though rare in variceal); assess for aspiration | Any infiltrate should prompt antibiotics |
| **Urgent endoscopy (OGD)** | Identify varices, active spurting or oozing, white nipple sign, red wale marks | Confirms diagnosis AND allows therapy (band ligation, glue for gastric varices) | Presence of varices + active bleeding or high-risk stigmata = immediate treatment; absence of varices suggests other cause |

**Single confirming finding:** At OGD – a bleeding varix (spurting, oozing, or with white nipple sign) is diagnostic.

**Pattern-recognition rule-out:** Absence of varices on OGD done by skilled endoscopist within 12 hours effectively rules out variceal bleeding as cause.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – what + why:**

1. **ABCs:** Protect airway (intubate if massive hematemesis, GCS <8, ongoing vomiting) – risk of aspiration is high.
2. **IV access:** Two large-bore cannulae (14-16G) or central line. If peripheral fails -> femoral or subclavian.
3. **Resuscitation:** Restrictive volume replacement – aim Hb 7-8 g/dL, MAP >65 mmHg. **Why too much crystalloid increases portal pressure and rebleeding risk.** Use packed red cells, avoid excessive saline. If coagulopathy: FFP only if INR >2 (controversial; some guidelines reserve for massive transfusion). Platelets if <50 with active bleeding.
4. **Vasoactive drug:** **Terlipressin** 2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg IV q4-6h for up to 5 days. **Why:** Splanchnic vasoconstrictor reduces portal pressure. Alternative: **Octreotide** 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/h infusion (but less evidence than terlipressin). Start BEFORE endoscopy.
5. **Antibiotics:** **Ceftriaxone** 1 g IV daily for 5-7 days (or third-gen cephalosporin). **Why:** Prevents spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and reduces rebleeding and mortality in cirrhotics.
6. **Correct coagulopathy if severe:** Vitamin K 10 mg IV if INR prolonged due to vitamin K deficiency (common in cirrhosis). Consider **Tranexamic acid**? HALT-IT trial showed no benefit for UGI bleed (including variceal) – **do not use routinely**.
7. **Place NGT?** Controversial – may help gastric lavage to improve endoscopic view, but can increase risk of migration of clot. Better to do endoscopy directly. Avoid routine NGT placement.

**Ward (24–72 h) – what + why:**

- **Continue terlipressin** for up to 5 days (reduces rebleed within 5 days).
- **Endoscopy** as soon as patient is haemodynamically stable (ideally within 12 h). Perform band ligation for oesophageal varices; for gastric varices – glue (N-butyl cyanoacrylate) or banding if small.
- **After endoscopy:** check for rebleeding signs (haematemesis, tachycardia, hypotension, melena). Monitor haemoglobin q8h.
- **Proton pump inhibitor (PPI):** Omeprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/h infusion for first 72 h is controversial – commonly used irrespective of aetiology because ulcer vs variceal is not always clear. Once variceal source confirmed, stop or de-escalate.
- **Secondary prophylaxis:** Start **non-selective beta-blocker** (propranolol 40 mg BD, titrate to HR 55-60, max 320 mg/day; or carvedilol 6.25-12.5 mg OD). **Why:** reduces HVPG, lowers rebleeding risk.
- **Serial endoscopy** until varices eradicated.

**Long-term – what + why:**

- **Beta-blocker continued** lifelong for secondary prophylaxis.
- **Treat underlying liver disease** – alcohol abstinence, antiviral therapy (Hep B/C), weight loss/lifestyle for NAFLD, autoimmune hepatitis immunosuppression.
- **Repeat EGD** every 1-3 years depending on varices severity.
- **If bleeding recurs despite beta-blocker and banding** – consider **TIPS** (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) to reduce portal pressure.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- NOT giving antibiotics – big mistake.
- Over-resuscitation with crystalloids (increase portal pressure).
- Starting PPI and painkillers without thinking of variceal source (PPI doesn't help varices; NSAIDs worsen).
- Using octreotide instead of terlipressin as first line – terlipressin is superior for rebleeding.
- Failing to start beta-blocker before discharge – it halves rebleeding risk.
- Not checking INR and correcting early.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Variceal Bleed | Peptic Ulcer | Mallory-Weiss Tear | Gastritis (erosive) |
|---------|----------------|--------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Pain** | Painless | Epigastric pain (often burning, relieved by food/antacids) | Retrosternal epigastric pain after retching | Mild epigastric discomfort |
| **Vomiting history** | Often none | Maybe vomiting due to pain | Preceded by non-bloody vomiting/retching (alcohol binge) | Possibly vomiting from gastritis |
| **Bleeding pattern** | Massive hematemesis (bright red), often without warning | Hematemesis or melena, often moderate | Bright red streaks after violent retching | Oozing, coffee-ground emesis, melena |
| **Risk factors** | Liver disease, portal HTN | NSAIDs, H. pylori, stress, smoking | Binge drinking, bulimia | NSAIDs, alcohol, stress, H. pylori |
| **Physical exam** | Stigmata of cirrhosis (spiders, ascites, hepatomegaly), melena | Abdominal tenderness, may have melena | No stigmata, epigastric tenderness | No specific signs |
| **Endoscopy** | Varices, red wale sign, nipple sign | Ulcer crater with visible vessel or clot | Linear mucosal tear at gastro-oesophageal junction | Diffuse erythema, petechiae, friability |
| **Key distinguishing factor** | Known liver disease + bright red hematemesis + shock | Pain + NSAID use; ulcer on OGD | Retching first | Endoscopy shows diffuse inflammation; no varices/ulcer |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Exsanguination:** Rapid death if not treated. Presents with persistent hypotension, massive ongoing hematemesis, loss of consciousness. **What to do:** massive transfusion protocol, emergency banding, balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as bridge to TIPS.
- **Aspiration pneumonia:** Especially if not intubated. Fever, hypoxia, new CXR infiltrate. **Key signs:** Tachypnoea, frothy or bloody sputum, decreased saturations. **Action:** Start antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole), consider bronchoscopy.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** Blood in gut → ammonia overload → confusion, asterixis, coma. **Key signs:** reversal of sleep-wake cycle, asterixis, confusion, Glasgow Coma Scale drop. **Action:** Lactulose 30 mL q1-2h until passage of 2-3 soft stools; rifaximin if refractory; avoid benzodiazepines.
- **Rebleeding within 5 days:** Highest risk period. Presents as recurrence of hematemesis/melena + haemodynamic instability. **Action:** Repeat endoscopy with banding; if rebleed despite two sessions, refer for TIPS.
- **Bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** Up to 30% of variceal bleeders develop SBP. **Key signs:** fever, abdominal pain, increased ascites, leukocytosis. **Action:** Diagnostic paracentesis (ascitic fluid PMN >250 cells/µL). Treat with ceftriaxone + albumin.

**Late presentation patterns – what happens if untreated:**
Patient presents a week later with massive ascites, hyponatraemia, marked jaundice, coagulopathy, and confusion. This is acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) precipitated by a variceal bleed – very high mortality.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **V-A-R-I-C-E-S** (management steps for acute bleed):
  - **V** – Vasoactive drug (terlipressin)
  - **A** – Antibiotics (ceftriaxone)
  - **R** – Resuscitate (restrictive)
  - **I** – Investigate (urgent OGD)
  - **C** – Correct coagulopathy (vitamin K, FFP if massive)
  - **E** – Endoscopic band ligation (or glue)
  - **S** – Secondary prophylaxis (beta-blocker)

- **B-S-S** (to remember three key drug classes):
  - **B** – Beta-blockers (secondary prevention)
  - **S** – Somatostatin analogues / terlipressin (acute)
  - **S** – Systemic antibiotics (ceftriaxone)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **Terlipressin is contraindicated in patients with coronary artery disease or severe hypertension** – use octreotide instead.
2. **Balloon tamponade is a rescue, not a definitive treatment** – can cause oesophageal rupture (if misplaced or >24h). Never leave without checking placement with CXR.
3. **Gastric varices (especially fundal) are harder to treat** – often require glue injection, TIPS, or balloon-occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO).
4. **Platelet count <50 predicts higher rebleeding** – consider platelet transfusion during active bleeding if count <50.
5. **In cirrhosis, Hb 7-8 g/dL is target** – higher Hb can increase portal pressure due to increased blood viscosity.
6. **Sengstaken-Blakemore tube has three ports** – gastric balloon, esophageal balloon, and gastric aspiration. Deflate esophageal balloon first before removal.
7. **Do not use nasogastric tube sampling to test for "coffee ground" in suspected variceal bleed** – it can precipitate rebleeding.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (SpR / Consultant):**
- Haemodynamically unstable despite 2 units of blood (MAP <65, HR >100)
- Active hematemesis with clot vomiting (>500 mL)
- Suspected gastric variceal bleed (fundal)
- Unable to control bleeding by banding at endoscopy (consider TIPS)
- Rebleeding within 24 hours after initial endoscopy
- Child-Pugh C cirrhosis (high risk of decompensation)
- Need for balloon tamponade

**When to call ICU / Crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (sustained tachycardia >130, BP <80 systolic, respiratory rate >25, O2 sat <90%, GCS <13)
- Lactate >4 mmol/L despite resuscitation
- GCS ≤8 (consider airway protection)
- Need for vasopressors (noradrenaline) to maintain MAP >65
- Massive transfusion protocol triggered (ongoing bleeding, >4 units RBCs in 1 hour)
- Severe coagulopathy (INR >3, fibrinogen <1 g/L) not correctable

**Specific thresholds for escalation:**
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → call crash team if systolic <80 mmHg after two IV cannulae and fluid challenge
- **Suspected rebleed** → if hemoglobin drops >2 g/dL in 4 hours or new haematemesis, call SpR immediately
- **pH <7.2** on ABG (metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion) → ICU for central monitoring and possible intubation

**Bottom line:** Variceal bleeding is a time-critical emergency. Know your drugs (terlipressin + ceftriaxone), know when to call for endoscopic help, and when to escalate to TIPS. Mortality halved if you follow evidence-based protocols. You've got this – just don't let the patient drown in saline.

rawMarkdown:
## Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
**One-liner:** Life-threatening UGI bleed from ruptured esophageal/gastric varices due to portal hypertension; requires immediate resuscitation and endoscopic therapy.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 52-year-old male with known alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) presents to ED with massive hematemesis (two cups of bright red blood). He is pale, sweaty, and restless. Heart rate 115 bpm, BP 85/55, saturations 96% on air. Abdominal exam reveals mild ascites and a firm liver edge. Skin shows spider naevi and palmar erythema. He has not had an endoscopy before. On rectal exam – melena.

**Dissect the vignette:**

- **Massive hematemesis (bright red blood)** → high-volume arterial-like bleeding from high-pressure variceal source (not oozing). Bright red implies fresh, not altered by gastric acid – suggests ongoing active bleeding.
- **Known alcoholic cirrhosis** → the underlying cause of portal hypertension – varices are present in ~50% of cirrhotics at diagnosis.
- **Hemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension)** → hypovolemic shock from acute blood loss – needs immediate IV access and resuscitation.
- **Ascites + firm liver edge** → signs of portal hypertension and chronic liver disease (fibrosis). Ascites indicates decompensation – worse prognosis.
- **Spider naevi / palmar erythema** → stigmata of chronic liver disease – confirm portal hypertensive aetiology.
- **Melena** → blood has transited through the GI tract – but here massive hematemesis dominates. Even with upper GI bleed, rapid transit can produce melena.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Only melena, no hematemesis** – can happen if bleeding is slow or intermittent, or if the varices are gastric (fundal). The junior doctor might focus on lower GI source – always consider UGI in any melena.
- **Patient with no known liver disease** – first presentation of cirrhosis can be variceal bleeding. No stigmata on exam. Be alert for history of alcohol use, hepatitis, obesity; check LFTs and platelet count (low platelets suggest portal hypertension / hypersplenism).
- **Bleeding after non-invasive procedure (e.g., PEG insertion, endoscopy for other indication)** – acute increase in portal pressure due to sedation or straining can rupture a previously silent varix. Don't dismiss as just "post-procedure ooze."

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
Variceal upper GI bleeding is a severe hemorrhagic event from dilated, tortuous veins (varices) in the oesophagus or stomach that develop because of high blood pressure in the portal venous system (portal hypertension). These veins are fragile and prone to rupture, leading to massive, life-threatening blood loss.

**Why it matters clinically – frequency, stakes, common pitfalls:**

- **High stakes:** 6-week mortality of variceal bleeding is 15–25% per episode. Each rebleed event worsens survival. It is the second most common cause of upper GI bleed after peptic ulcer, but far more dangerous.
- **Frequency:** Most common in patients with cirrhosis (global burden: ~2 million deaths/year from cirrhosis, ~30% will have varices; 30% will bleed at some point). Also seen in extrahepatic portal vein thrombosis.
- **Common pitfalls:** Delayed fluid resuscitation (over-resuscitation worsens portal pressure); failing to start vasoactive drugs immediately (before endoscopy); forgetting antibiotics (reduce infection and rebleeding); not considering TIPS early in uncontrolled bleeding.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**

Portal hypertension is defined as a hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) >10 mmHg. In cirrhosis, fibrosis and regenerative nodules compress hepatic sinusoids, increasing intrahepatic resistance. Additionally, splanchnic vasodilation (due to nitric oxide, VEGF) increases portal blood flow, exacerbating hypertension. Over time, the body tries to decompress the portal system by opening collateral vessels – gastroesophageal (coronary vein → azygos), splenorenal, caput medusae. These collaterals are thin-walled and lack smooth muscle. As HVPG exceeds 12 mmHg, varices develop; at HVPG >16 mmHg, they are at high risk of rupture.

**Mechanism → sign:**

- **Rupture → hematemesis / melena:** Increased wall tension (LaPlace's law: T = P × r / thickness). Larger varices (red wale signs) rupture more easily. Acid reflux + mechanical trauma from swallowing can erode the overlying mucosa.
- **Hemodynamic instability:** Acute loss of 30–40% circulating volume → compensatory tachycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries. Liver disease blunts the normal vascular response – worse shock.
- **Ascites and spider naevi:** Portal hypertension increases hydrostatic pressure in splanchnic capillaries, causing fluid shift into peritoneal cavity. Spider naevi are caused by oestrogen accumulation (liver can't metabolise) leading to dilated arterioles – marker of chronicity.
- **Thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism:** Splenic congestion → sequestration of platelets – low platelet count is a clue to portal hypertension before varices are visible.
- **Encephalopathy:** Blood in the gut is a nitrogen load – bacteria produce ammonia, which crosses the blood-brain barrier → impaired consciousness, asterixis. This can worsen after bleeding.

**Connect to pattern cues:**
Patient with cirrhosis + melena + no pain → think variceal (painless massive bleed). Peptic ulcer often has EPIGASTRIC PAIN. Mallory-Weiss has history of retching.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Chronic liver disease (most common)** | Cirrhosis (alcoholic, viral hepatitis B/C, NAFLD, autoimmune, PBC, PSC) | Known liver disease, stigmata, abnormal LFTs, low platelets |
| **Extrahepatic portal vein obstruction** | Portal vein thrombosis (neonatal, pancreatitis, hypercoagulable, myeloproliferative) | Present with variceal bleed but normal liver function; splenomegaly out of proportion |
| **Pre-hepatic (splenic vein thrombosis)** | Pancreatitis, pancreatic tumour → isolated left-sided (gastric) varices | Oesophageal varices may be absent; gastric fundal varices; normal liver |
| **Post-hepatic (Budd-Chiari, VOD)** | Hepatic vein outflow obstruction (hypercoagulable, myeloproliferative) | Hepatomegaly, ascites, acute onset with elevated transaminases; liver may not be cirrhotic initially |
| **Triggers of rupture** | Increased portal pressure (straining, ascites paracentesis, constipation, heavy meal); infection (SBP); NSAIDs/aspirin; alcohol binge | Recent event that raised intra-abdominal pressure or caused mucosal damage |

**Most common cause:** Alcoholic cirrhosis (or now NAFLD rising).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic scoring:** Use the **Rockall score** (pre-endoscopy: age, shock, comorbidities) or **GBS (Glasgow-Blatchford)** to predict need for intervention. But for varices specifically – **endoscopy** is the gold standard diagnostic and therapeutic tool.

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Full blood count** | Haemoglobin, platelets | Assess severity of anaemia, detect thrombocytopenia suggesting hypersplenism | Hb drop of >2 g/dL = substantial bleed; Platelet <100 = likely portal HTN |
| **Urea & creatinine** | Raised urea relative to creatinine (with normal renal function) indicates blood in gut (urea load from protein digestion) | Differentiates upper vs lower GI bleed – uraemia from upper bleed | BUN/Cr ratio >30:1 suggests UGI source (not specific but helpful in context) |
| **LFTs + INR** | Albumin low, bilirubin elevated, INR prolonged | Assess liver synthetic function – guides prognosis (Child-Pugh); high INR increases bleeding risk and is a marker of severity | INR >2 = risk of rebleeding; Child-Pugh C = poor prognosis |
| **Chest X-ray** | Aspiration pneumonitis, free air | Rule out perforation (though rare in variceal); assess for aspiration | Any infiltrate should prompt antibiotics |
| **Urgent endoscopy (OGD)** | Identify varices, active spurting or oozing, white nipple sign, red wale marks | Confirms diagnosis AND allows therapy (band ligation, glue for gastric varices) | Presence of varices + active bleeding or high-risk stigmata = immediate treatment; absence of varices suggests other cause |

**Single confirming finding:** At OGD – a bleeding varix (spurting, oozing, or with white nipple sign) is diagnostic.

**Pattern-recognition rule-out:** Absence of varices on OGD done by skilled endoscopist within 12 hours effectively rules out variceal bleeding as cause.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – what + why:**

1. **ABCs:** Protect airway (intubate if massive hematemesis, GCS <8, ongoing vomiting) – risk of aspiration is high.
2. **IV access:** Two large-bore cannulae (14-16G) or central line. If peripheral fails -> femoral or subclavian.
3. **Resuscitation:** Restrictive volume replacement – aim Hb 7-8 g/dL, MAP >65 mmHg. **Why too much crystalloid increases portal pressure and rebleeding risk.** Use packed red cells, avoid excessive saline. If coagulopathy: FFP only if INR >2 (controversial; some guidelines reserve for massive transfusion). Platelets if <50 with active bleeding.
4. **Vasoactive drug:** **Terlipressin** 2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg IV q4-6h for up to 5 days. **Why:** Splanchnic vasoconstrictor reduces portal pressure. Alternative: **Octreotide** 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/h infusion (but less evidence than terlipressin). Start BEFORE endoscopy.
5. **Antibiotics:** **Ceftriaxone** 1 g IV daily for 5-7 days (or third-gen cephalosporin). **Why:** Prevents spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and reduces rebleeding and mortality in cirrhotics.
6. **Correct coagulopathy if severe:** Vitamin K 10 mg IV if INR prolonged due to vitamin K deficiency (common in cirrhosis). Consider **Tranexamic acid**? HALT-IT trial showed no benefit for UGI bleed (including variceal) – **do not use routinely**.
7. **Place NGT?** Controversial – may help gastric lavage to improve endoscopic view, but can increase risk of migration of clot. Better to do endoscopy directly. Avoid routine NGT placement.

**Ward (24–72 h) – what + why:**

- **Continue terlipressin** for up to 5 days (reduces rebleed within 5 days).
- **Endoscopy** as soon as patient is haemodynamically stable (ideally within 12 h). Perform band ligation for oesophageal varices; for gastric varices – glue (N-butyl cyanoacrylate) or banding if small.
- **After endoscopy:** check for rebleeding signs (haematemesis, tachycardia, hypotension, melena). Monitor haemoglobin q8h.
- **Proton pump inhibitor (PPI):** Omeprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/h infusion for first 72 h is controversial – commonly used irrespective of aetiology because ulcer vs variceal is not always clear. Once variceal source confirmed, stop or de-escalate.
- **Secondary prophylaxis:** Start **non-selective beta-blocker** (propranolol 40 mg BD, titrate to HR 55-60, max 320 mg/day; or carvedilol 6.25-12.5 mg OD). **Why:** reduces HVPG, lowers rebleeding risk.
- **Serial endoscopy** until varices eradicated.

**Long-term – what + why:**

- **Beta-blocker continued** lifelong for secondary prophylaxis.
- **Treat underlying liver disease** – alcohol abstinence, antiviral therapy (Hep B/C), weight loss/lifestyle for NAFLD, autoimmune hepatitis immunosuppression.
- **Repeat EGD** every 1-3 years depending on varices severity.
- **If bleeding recurs despite beta-blocker and banding** – consider **TIPS** (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) to reduce portal pressure.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- NOT giving antibiotics – big mistake.
- Over-resuscitation with crystalloids (increase portal pressure).
- Starting PPI and painkillers without thinking of variceal source (PPI doesn't help varices; NSAIDs worsen).
- Using octreotide instead of terlipressin as first line – terlipressin is superior for rebleeding.
- Failing to start beta-blocker before discharge – it halves rebleeding risk.
- Not checking INR and correcting early.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Variceal Bleed | Peptic Ulcer | Mallory-Weiss Tear | Gastritis (erosive) |
|---------|----------------|--------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Pain** | Painless | Epigastric pain (often burning, relieved by food/antacids) | Retrosternal epigastric pain after retching | Mild epigastric discomfort |
| **Vomiting history** | Often none | Maybe vomiting due to pain | Preceded by non-bloody vomiting/retching (alcohol binge) | Possibly vomiting from gastritis |
| **Bleeding pattern** | Massive hematemesis (bright red), often without warning | Hematemesis or melena, often moderate | Bright red streaks after violent retching | Oozing, coffee-ground emesis, melena |
| **Risk factors** | Liver disease, portal HTN | NSAIDs, H. pylori, stress, smoking | Binge drinking, bulimia | NSAIDs, alcohol, stress, H. pylori |
| **Physical exam** | Stigmata of cirrhosis (spiders, ascites, hepatomegaly), melena | Abdominal tenderness, may have melena | No stigmata, epigastric tenderness | No specific signs |
| **Endoscopy** | Varices, red wale sign, nipple sign | Ulcer crater with visible vessel or clot | Linear mucosal tear at gastro-oesophageal junction | Diffuse erythema, petechiae, friability |
| **Key distinguishing factor** | Known liver disease + bright red hematemesis + shock | Pain + NSAID use; ulcer on OGD | Retching first | Endoscopy shows diffuse inflammation; no varices/ulcer |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Exsanguination:** Rapid death if not treated. Presents with persistent hypotension, massive ongoing hematemesis, loss of consciousness. **What to do:** massive transfusion protocol, emergency banding, balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as bridge to TIPS.
- **Aspiration pneumonia:** Especially if not intubated. Fever, hypoxia, new CXR infiltrate. **Key signs:** Tachypnoea, frothy or bloody sputum, decreased saturations. **Action:** Start antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole), consider bronchoscopy.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** Blood in gut → ammonia overload → confusion, asterixis, coma. **Key signs:** reversal of sleep-wake cycle, asterixis, confusion, Glasgow Coma Scale drop. **Action:** Lactulose 30 mL q1-2h until passage of 2-3 soft stools; rifaximin if refractory; avoid benzodiazepines.
- **Rebleeding within 5 days:** Highest risk period. Presents as recurrence of hematemesis/melena + haemodynamic instability. **Action:** Repeat endoscopy with banding; if rebleed despite two sessions, refer for TIPS.
- **Bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** Up to 30% of variceal bleeders develop SBP. **Key signs:** fever, abdominal pain, increased ascites, leukocytosis. **Action:** Diagnostic paracentesis (ascitic fluid PMN >250 cells/µL). Treat with ceftriaxone + albumin.

**Late presentation patterns – what happens if untreated:**
Patient presents a week later with massive ascites, hyponatraemia, marked jaundice, coagulopathy, and confusion. This is acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) precipitated by a variceal bleed – very high mortality.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **V-A-R-I-C-E-S** (management steps for acute bleed):
  - **V** – Vasoactive drug (terlipressin)
  - **A** – Antibiotics (ceftriaxone)
  - **R** – Resuscitate (restrictive)
  - **I** – Investigate (urgent OGD)
  - **C** – Correct coagulopathy (vitamin K, FFP if massive)
  - **E** – Endoscopic band ligation (or glue)
  - **S** – Secondary prophylaxis (beta-blocker)

- **B-S-S** (to remember three key drug classes):
  - **B** – Beta-blockers (secondary prevention)
  - **S** – Somatostatin analogues / terlipressin (acute)
  - **S** – Systemic antibiotics (ceftriaxone)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **Terlipressin is contraindicated in patients with coronary artery disease or severe hypertension** – use octreotide instead.
2. **Balloon tamponade is a rescue, not a definitive treatment** – can cause oesophageal rupture (if misplaced or >24h). Never leave without checking placement with CXR.
3. **Gastric varices (especially fundal) are harder to treat** – often require glue injection, TIPS, or balloon-occluded retrograde transvenous obliteration (BRTO).
4. **Platelet count <50 predicts higher rebleeding** – consider platelet transfusion during active bleeding if count <50.
5. **In cirrhosis, Hb 7-8 g/dL is target** – higher Hb can increase portal pressure due to increased blood viscosity.
6. **Sengstaken-Blakemore tube has three ports** – gastric balloon, esophageal balloon, and gastric aspiration. Deflate esophageal balloon first before removal.
7. **Do not use nasogastric tube sampling to test for "coffee ground" in suspected variceal bleed** – it can precipitate rebleeding.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (SpR / Consultant):**
- Haemodynamically unstable despite 2 units of blood (MAP <65, HR >100)
- Active hematemesis with clot vomiting (>500 mL)
- Suspected gastric variceal bleed (fundal)
- Unable to control bleeding by banding at endoscopy (consider TIPS)
- Rebleeding within 24 hours after initial endoscopy
- Child-Pugh C cirrhosis (high risk of decompensation)
- Need for balloon tamponade

**When to call ICU / Crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (sustained tachycardia >130, BP <80 systolic, respiratory rate >25, O2 sat <90%, GCS <13)
- Lactate >4 mmol/L despite resuscitation
- GCS ≤8 (consider airway protection)
- Need for vasopressors (noradrenaline) to maintain MAP >65
- Massive transfusion protocol triggered (ongoing bleeding, >4 units RBCs in 1 hour)
- Severe coagulopathy (INR >3, fibrinogen <1 g/L) not correctable

**Specific thresholds for escalation:**
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → call crash team if systolic <80 mmHg after two IV cannulae and fluid challenge
- **Suspected rebleed** → if hemoglobin drops >2 g/dL in 4 hours or new haematemesis, call SpR immediately
- **pH <7.2** on ABG (metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion) → ICU for central monitoring and possible intubation

**Bottom line:** Variceal bleeding is a time-critical emergency. Know your drugs (terlipressin + ceftriaxone), know when to call for endoscopic help, and when to escalate to TIPS. Mortality halved if you follow evidence-based protocols. You've got this – just don't let the patient drown in saline.
